heroOverview: Hypokalemia — a serum potassium below 3.5 mmol/L — is one of the most common electrolyte abnormalities encountered in clinical medicine, affecting approximately 20% of hospitalised patients at some point during their admission. Potassium is essential for maintaining the resting membrane potential of muscle and nerve cells; a fall from the normal 3.5-5.0 mmol/L range progressively impairs cardiac conduction, skeletal muscle contraction, and intestinal motility. Even moderate hypokalemia below 3.0 mmol/L roughly doubles the risk of ventricular arrhythmia in patients with heart disease. The condition is most often caused by gastrointestinal losses, diuretic therapy, or transcellular shifts. In most cases it is correctable with targeted supplementation, but identifying the underlying cause is essential to prevent recurrence and guide the appropriate route and rate of potassium replacement.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 20% of hospitalised patients; present in up to 40% of patients on thiazide diuretics for hypertension and in over 50% of patients with eating disorders involving purging
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; most common in patients on diuretics, those with gastrointestinal losses, and people with eating disorders; women more often affected by diuretic and eating-disorder causes
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; risk is highest in adults over 60 on long-term diuretic therapy
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 5 million people in the United States have clinically significant hypokalemia at any given time; it is among the top 5 electrolyte abnormalities triggering emergency hospital admissions

definition: Hypokalemia (ICD-10: E87.6) is defined as a serum potassium concentration below 3.5 mmol/L, classified as mild (3.0-3.5 mmol/L), moderate (2.5-3.0 mmol/L), and severe (below 2.5 mmol/L). Potassium is the predominant intracellular cation: approximately 98% of total body potassium (3,500 mmol in a 70 kg adult) is stored within cells, primarily in muscle. The steep electrochemical gradient between intracellular potassium (approximately 140 mmol/L) and extracellular potassium (3.5-5.0 mmol/L) determines the resting membrane potential of excitable cells, calculated by the Nernst equation. When extracellular potassium falls, this gradient steepens, hyperpolarising the membrane and making it harder for muscle and nerve cells to fire. Physiological potassium balance is maintained primarily by the kidneys, which adjust excretion under the direction of aldosterone. Hypokalemia arises through three mechanisms: inadequate intake (rare in isolation, usually contributing alongside losses); increased urinary or gastrointestinal losses; or transcellular shift from plasma into cells. Urinary losses from diuretics and gastrointestinal losses from vomiting, diarrhoea, or nasogastric suction are the most common. The kidneys compensate by lowering urinary potassium excretion below 20 mmol/day; when hypokalemia coexists with urinary potassium above 20-30 mmol/day, the kidney is the source of losses and an intrinsic renal or hormonal cause must be sought. Hypokalemia carries elevated cardiovascular risk: meta-analyses estimate each 1 mmol/L fall in potassium below 4.0 mmol/L is associated with a 10% increase in ventricular arrhythmia risk in patients with underlying heart disease.

typesClassification.description: Often asymptomatic or produces mild fatigue and muscle cramps; ECG may show subtle T-wave flattening; manageable with oral supplementation and dietary adjustment.
More consistent muscle weakness, constipation, and notable ECG changes including prominent U waves; oral supplementation and addressing the underlying cause are usually sufficient.

Risk of paralysis, rhabdomyolysis, dangerous arrhythmia (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation), ileus, and — in extreme cases — respiratory failure; often requires intravenous potassium replacement and hospital admission.

The most common type in clinical practice; loop (furosemide) and thiazide (hydrochlorothiazide, bendroflumethiazide) diuretics increase distal tubular sodium delivery, driving potassium secretion. Accounts for approximately 40% of hypokalemia cases in outpatients.

A rare genetic (CACNA1A mutations) or thyrotoxicosis-associated disorder where dramatic transcellular potassium shifts cause episodic profound weakness or paralysis, typically provoked by carbohydrate-rich meals, insulin, or exercise.

primarySymptoms: Muscle weakness — the most common and clinically important symptom, typically proximal (hips and thighs before hands), caused by hyperpolarisation of muscle membranes that impairs action potential generation.
Muscle cramps and aching — experienced as painful muscle spasms or a diffuse deep aching sensation, particularly in the legs, especially at night and during exertion.
Constipation and abdominal bloating — hypokalemia slows intestinal smooth muscle contraction; severe cases produce a frank paralytic ileus with absent bowel sounds and abdominal distension.

Palpitations and irregular heartbeat — ventricular ectopic beats, which may feel like missed or extra beats, are common in moderate to severe hypokalemia as cardiac myocyte membrane excitability is altered.
Fatigue and generalised weakness — a persistent energy deficit, distinct from localised muscle weakness, reflects widespread effects on skeletal muscle and cellular metabolism.
Polyuria and polydipsia — chronic hypokalemia impairs renal tubular concentrating ability (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus-like effect), causing excessive urine output and corresponding thirst.
Nausea and vomiting — both as a consequence of gastrointestinal dysmotility from hypokalemia and, in a reinforcing cycle, as a cause of ongoing potassium loss.
Numbness and tingling (paraesthesia) — altered peripheral nerve membrane potential causes abnormal sensory symptoms, particularly in the extremities.
Flat or inverted T waves and prominent U waves on ECG — the characteristic electrocardiographic signature of moderate to severe hypokalemia, reflecting altered cardiac repolarisation.
Respiratory weakness — in severe cases (potassium below 2.0 mmol/L), the diaphragm and respiratory muscles may become sufficiently weak to require ventilatory support.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset leg cramps or nocturnal muscle cramps in a patient on thiazide or loop diuretics.
Mild fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance that develops gradually in a patient on long-term diuretic therapy.
Increased frequency of urination with thirst in a patient not known to have diabetes, suggesting potassium-related impairment of urine-concentrating ability.
Occasional palpitations or awareness of irregular heartbeat in a patient with known cardiac disease.
emergencySigns: Serum potassium below 2.5 mmol/L with weakness, paralysis, or respiratory difficulty — requires hospital admission and intravenous potassium replacement.
ECG changes including torsades de pointes, prolonged QT interval, ventricular tachycardia, or ventricular fibrillation in the context of low potassium.
Complete muscle paralysis of the extremities or trunk progressing to breathing difficulty — suggests hypokalemic periodic paralysis or extreme depletion.
Paralytic ileus with abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, and vomiting in the setting of severe hypokalemia.

causes.description: Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide) and thiazides (hydrochlorothiazide, bendroflumethiazide) increase delivery of sodium to the distal collecting duct, where the Na/K exchanger accelerates potassium excretion. A 40 mg dose of furosemide can increase urinary potassium loss by 30-60 mmol per day, rapidly depleting total body stores if dietary intake is insufficient.

causes.cause: Gastrointestinal losses — vomiting and nasogastric drainage
causes.description: Vomiting causes hypokalemia primarily through metabolic alkalosis rather than direct potassium loss in vomitus: alkalosis increases renal potassium wasting, and volume depletion activates aldosterone, further driving urinary potassium loss. Nasogastric suction and pyloric obstruction create the same mechanism, sometimes producing severe hypokalemia with profound alkalosis.

Unlike vomiting, diarrhoea causes direct faecal potassium loss; stool potassium content ranges from 30-140 mmol/L. Secretory diarrhoeas — cholera, VIPoma, villous adenoma — can deplete 200-400 mmol of potassium daily, creating rapidly life-threatening hypokalemia with concomitant metabolic acidosis.

Excess aldosterone from an adrenal adenoma (Conn's syndrome), bilateral hyperplasia, or secondary stimulation (heart failure, renovascular hypertension) drives inappropriate renal potassium wasting. Conn's syndrome produces hypertension, persistent hypokalemia, and a suppressed plasma renin; it is the most common cause of resistant hypertension at approximately 5-10% of all hypertension cases.

Insulin stimulates Na/K-ATPase, shifting potassium into cells; post-meal insulin surges after high-carbohydrate intake cause transient hypokalaemia. Beta-2 adrenergic stimulation from endogenous catecholamines (stress, myocardial infarction) or exogenous salbutamol/albuterol produces the same effect. In thyrotoxicosis, excess catecholamines underlie hypokalemic periodic paralysis.

While the kidneys can reduce potassium excretion to as little as 5-10 mmol/day, sustained inadequate intake from starvation, eating disorders, or restricted parenteral nutrition eventually depletes total body stores. The combination of poor intake with any concurrent loss is the most common clinical scenario in malnourished patients.

Distal (type 1) and proximal (type 2) RTA impair normal acid excretion and potassium reabsorption mechanisms in the tubule. Both cause hypokalemia with a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis; type 1 RTA is particularly likely to produce severe hypokalemia because it stimulates aldosterone as a secondary consequence of volume contraction.

riskFactors.description: The single strongest independent risk factor for hypokalemia in outpatients; potassium monitoring after initiation and dose change is standard practice. Adding a potassium-sparing agent or ensuring adequate dietary potassium intake reduces the risk of deficiency by approximately 50%.

The reference dietary intake for potassium is 3,500-4,700 mg (90-120 mmol) per day; diets poor in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains fall well short of this, reducing the buffer against any additional losses from medications or illness.

Self-induced vomiting and laxative abuse are among the most severe causes of potassium depletion; more than 50% of patients with purging-type bulimia have hypokalemia. Dental enamel erosion and parotid swelling are other clues. Safe replacement requires psychiatric co-management.

An aldosterone-secreting adrenal adenoma or bilateral hyperplasia drives continuous renal potassium wasting; this diagnosis should be considered in any hypertensive patient with persistent unprovoked hypokalemia, especially if resistant to standard antihypertensives.

riskFactors.factor: Gastrointestinal illness with prolonged vomiting or diarrhoea
riskFactors.description: Any disease causing significant gastrointestinal fluid losses — inflammatory bowel disease, infectious gastroenteritis, post-surgical drainage — depletes potassium rapidly; monitoring and replacement during prolonged illness are essential.

Insulin therapy drives potassium into cells; frequent hypoglycaemia-insulin oscillations, renal glucose losses, and the tendency to restrict potassium-rich foods in diabetes management all contribute to chronic mild hypokalemia in this population.

Hypomagnesaemia independently causes renal potassium wasting by impairing Na/K-ATPase function in tubular cells; hypokalemia cannot be reliably corrected until magnesium is also replenished. This is a frequently overlooked reason for refractory hypokalemia.

riskFactors.factor: High ambient temperature or intense prolonged exercise
riskFactors.description: Sweat contains approximately 5-15 mmol/L of potassium; during prolonged exercise or heat stress, sweat losses of 1-3 litres per hour can deplete several hundred millimoles of potassium within hours, particularly if only plain water is consumed for replacement.

affectedDemographics: Patients on long-term thiazide or loop diuretics for hypertension, heart failure, or oedema
Elderly adults over 65 with multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy involving diuretics
Young women with eating disorders involving purging behaviours
Patients with inflammatory bowel disease, short bowel syndrome, or chronic diarrhoeal illness
Patients with primary hyperaldosteronism or adrenal adenoma
People with thyrotoxicosis, particularly those prone to hypokalemic periodic paralysis
Athletes participating in prolonged endurance exercise in hot environments
Critically ill patients with nasogastric suction, post-surgical losses, or receiving high-dose insulin infusions
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is established on a serum potassium below 3.5 mmol/L. The key step after confirmation is identifying the mechanism — urinary potassium excretion distinguishes renal causes from extra-renal causes. A 24-hour urine potassium below 20 mmol/day (or spot urine potassium-to-creatinine ratio below 13 mmol/mmol) indicates appropriate renal conservation and localises losses to the gastrointestinal tract or transcellular shift. Urinary potassium above 20-30 mmol/day despite hypokalemia indicates inappropriate renal wasting — the kidney is the source of losses. In this setting, acid-base status directs the differential: metabolic alkalosis with elevated urinary potassium points toward vomiting, loop or thiazide diuretics, Bartter syndrome, or hyperaldosteronism; metabolic acidosis with urinary potassium wasting points toward renal tubular acidosis or diabetic ketoacidosis treatment. Plasma aldosterone and renin levels are measured when hyperaldosteronism is suspected; an aldosterone-to-renin ratio above 30-40 with a suppressed renin is a screening positive for primary hyperaldosteronism, triggering adrenal CT and, if needed, adrenal vein sampling. A thyroid function test is ordered when hypokalemic periodic paralysis is suspected, particularly in Asian patients. The ECG should be checked in all patients with moderate to severe hypokalemia; U waves (a positive deflection after the T wave, most prominent in V2-V4), T-wave flattening, ST depression, and prolonged QT are the characteristic findings. In patients with concurrent hypomagnesaemia — which occurs in approximately 40-60% of cases of hypokalemia — magnesium must be corrected simultaneously because potassium replacement alone will fail while magnesium remains low.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm hypokalemia, assess concurrent hypomagnesaemia (present in 40-60% of cases and impeding potassium correction), and provide context for acid-base interpretation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venepuncture; results within 1-2 hours. A haemolysed sample raises apparent potassium, so the test may need repeating if the result seems inconsistent with the clinical picture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect cardiac toxicity — U waves, T-wave flattening, QT prolongation, and ventricular arrhythmia — guiding urgency of treatment and monitoring during replacement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10 electrodes attached to limbs and chest; takes 5 minutes and produces a tracing reviewed immediately. Repeated during IV replacement to monitor improvement.

diagnosticTests.test: Arterial blood gas or venous bicarbonate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Determine acid-base status to narrow the differential — metabolic alkalosis favours GI losses or diuretics; metabolic acidosis favours diarrhoea or renal tubular acidosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An arterial blood draw from the wrist; results in minutes. A venous bicarbonate from the same blood draw can be used as an alternative where arterial access is impractical.

diagnosticTests.test: Spot urine potassium-to-creatinine ratio or 24-hour urine potassium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguish renal from extra-renal potassium losses; a ratio above 13 mmol/mmol (or 24-hour urinary potassium above 20-30 mmol) indicates inappropriate renal wasting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A midstream urine sample collected in a container; calculated using simultaneous serum values. A 24-hour urine collection requires a special container and precise timing over a full day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for primary hyperaldosteronism when hypokalemia is associated with hypertension and is persistent or difficult to replace; an elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio identifies this potentially surgically curable cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw under standardised conditions (upright posture, off aldosterone-affecting medications); results take several days at most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify thyrotoxicosis as the driver of hypokalemic periodic paralysis, particularly in East Asian patients presenting with episodic weakness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for TSH and free T4; results within 24 hours in most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect rhabdomyolysis as a consequence of severe hypokalemia, particularly in patients with profound weakness below a potassium of 2.0 mmol/L; elevated CK confirms muscle breakdown and indicates more aggressive replacement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn simultaneously with the electrolyte panel; result within 2-3 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment requires two simultaneous tasks: replacing the potassium deficit and correcting the underlying cause. Potassium deficit estimation guides the total replacement needed: each 1 mmol/L fall in serum potassium below 3.5 mmol/L corresponds roughly to a 200-400 mmol total body deficit, though this relationship is imprecise. For mild to moderate hypokalemia (2.5-3.5 mmol/L) in patients who can tolerate oral intake, oral potassium chloride (KCl) supplementation is first-line: 40-80 mmol orally per day in divided doses corrects most cases within 2-5 days. Potassium chloride is preferred over potassium citrate or bicarbonate when metabolic alkalosis is present, as chloride corrects the concurrent alkalosis. Increasing dietary potassium from fruits, vegetables, and dairy provides supportive supplementation but is rarely sufficient alone in moderate deficiency. Intravenous potassium is reserved for severe hypokalemia (below 2.5 mmol/L), for patients unable to take oral therapy, or for those with dangerous cardiac arrhythmia. IV potassium must be given slowly — standard guidance limits peripheral infusion to 10-20 mmol/hour at concentrations of 40 mmol/L or less to avoid local vein irritation and the risk of rebound hyperkalemia and cardiac toxicity if infused too rapidly. In central lines, up to 40 mmol/hour may be given with continuous cardiac monitoring. Magnesium deficiency is corrected simultaneously, as hypokalemia is refractory to replacement while magnesium is low. For diuretic-induced hypokalemia, the options include dose reduction of the diuretic, addition of a potassium-sparing diuretic (spironolactone, amiloride, eplerenone), and dietary optimisation. For primary hyperaldosteronism, surgical adrenalectomy resolves the hypokalemia permanently in those with a unilateral adenoma; bilateral disease is managed medically with mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists. For hypokalemic periodic paralysis, acute episodes are treated with oral or IV potassium; acetazolamide or dichlorphenamide may prevent episodes in genetic forms.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral potassium chloride supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Potassium chloride tablets, effervescent tablets, or liquid given 40-80 mmol daily in divided doses; first-line for mild to moderate hypokalemia in patients who can tolerate oral intake. Chloride co-replacement simultaneously corrects the metabolic alkalosis often associated with diuretic or GI-loss hypokalemia. Gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, bloating) are common with higher doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects mild to moderate hypokalemia within 2-5 days in approximately 85-90% of outpatient cases; slower in patients with ongoing losses requiring simultaneous cause treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous potassium chloride infusion
medicalTreatments.description: IV KCl given at 10-20 mmol/hour via peripheral vein (maximum 40 mmol/hour via central line with cardiac monitoring) for severe hypokalemia or when oral therapy is impossible. Each 10 mmol raises serum potassium by approximately 0.1 mmol/L. Repeated monitoring every 2-4 hours prevents overshoot hyperkalemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises serum potassium by approximately 0.1 mmol/L per 10 mmol infused; normalisation of severe hypokalemia (below 2.5 mmol/L) typically requires 100-200 mmol over 24-48 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride, eplerenone)
medicalTreatments.description: Added to loop or thiazide regimens to offset urinary potassium losses; spironolactone and eplerenone block aldosterone receptors, while amiloride blocks the epithelial sodium channel directly. Reduce ongoing urinary potassium wasting by 30-50%, often eliminating the need for supplemental potassium in mild cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spironolactone added to a thiazide diuretic reduces the incidence of hypokalemia by approximately 50% compared with thiazide alone in multiple randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Magnesium repletion (IV or oral magnesium)
medicalTreatments.description: Hypomagnesaemia is corrected before or alongside potassium to enable effective replacement; IV magnesium sulphate (1-2 g over 30-60 minutes) is used in hospital; oral magnesium oxide or glycinate is used for outpatient correction. Without magnesium correction, potassium leaks from cells continuously regardless of how much is given.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Correcting hypomagnesaemia from a mean of 0.6 mmol/L to above 0.85 mmol/L restores potassium-retention capacity and reduces the total potassium replacement dose needed by approximately 30-40%.

medicalTreatments.name: Adrenalectomy for primary hyperaldosteronism
medicalTreatments.description: Laparoscopic removal of a unilateral aldosterone-secreting adenoma is the definitive curative treatment; after surgery, hypokalemia resolves in essentially all patients and hypertension resolves or significantly improves in 30-70% of cases, reducing long-term cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves hypokalemia in approximately 100% of patients with confirmed unilateral adenoma; blood pressure normalises without medication in 35-60% of surgical candidates.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy for aldosteronoma
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive removal of a unilateral adrenal adenoma causing primary hyperaldosteronism and persistent hypokalemia. Indicated after adrenal CT and adrenal vein sampling confirm unilateral excess aldosterone secretion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves hypokalemia in approximately 100% of cases; blood pressure normalises in 35-60% and substantially improves in another 30%; operative mortality is below 0.5% at experienced centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary potassium optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing fruits (bananas, kiwi, prunes), vegetables (potatoes, sweet potatoes, spinach), legumes, and dairy can contribute 2,000-3,000 mg (50-75 mmol) of potassium daily; adequate for prevention but insufficient alone in established moderate deficiency.

alternativeTreatments.name: Electrolyte drinks for exercise-associated hypokalemia
alternativeTreatments.description: Sports drinks containing potassium and sodium replace sweat losses during prolonged exercise; appropriate for prevention and mild depletion in athletes but not a substitute for medical treatment in symptomatic cases.

whySeeSpecialist: Most diuretic-induced mild hypokalemia can be managed in primary care with dose adjustment and supplementation. A nephrologist should be consulted for persistent refractory hypokalemia, suspected renal tubular acidosis, or Bartter syndrome. An endocrinologist should evaluate patients with hypertension plus persistent hypokalemia for primary hyperaldosteronism, and thyrotoxicosis-associated hypokalemic periodic paralysis. Cardiologists should be involved in patients with severe hypokalemia complicated by arrhythmia.
doctorSelectionGuide: A clinician experienced in managing electrolyte disorders and able to order and interpret renin-aldosterone panels, renal tubular function tests, and acid-base status is needed for complex or refractory cases. Ask whether the practice has access to adrenal vein sampling for suspected primary hyperaldosteronism, as this procedure is only available at specialist centres and is critical before surgical decisions are made.
preventionStrategies: Monitor serum potassium within 2-4 weeks of starting or increasing thiazide or loop diuretic doses; check every 3-6 months on stable long-term therapy.
Consider adding a potassium-sparing diuretic or ACE inhibitor/ARB when persistent hypokalemia on a thiazide cannot be managed with dietary measures alone.
Eat 5 portions of fruits and vegetables daily to maintain a dietary potassium intake above 3,500 mg/day.
Replace electrolytes specifically during prolonged vomiting, diarrhoea, or intense exercise lasting over 90 minutes.
Correct concurrent hypomagnesaemia promptly, as low magnesium prevents effective potassium retention.
Screen patients with eating disorders for electrolyte abnormalities regularly, as hypokalaemia-related cardiac events are a major cause of death in these conditions.
lifestyleModifications: Include potassium-rich foods at every meal: aim for at least two servings of fruit and two servings of vegetables daily.
Avoid crash diets or very low-calorie diets without medical supervision, as these dramatically reduce potassium intake.
During illness with vomiting or diarrhoea, start oral electrolyte replacement early rather than waiting for symptoms to worsen.
If using laxatives, be aware of the potassium-depleting effect and discuss with a clinician if use is frequent or prolonged.
Avoid very large amounts of liquorice or liquorice extract, which contain glycyrrhizinic acid that mimics aldosterone, causing renal potassium wasting in susceptible individuals.
For athletes training intensively, consume electrolyte-containing drinks rather than plain water during exercise lasting more than 60-90 minutes.

recommended: Bananas, avocados, kiwi fruit, prunes, and oranges — all high in potassium
Potatoes (especially baked with skin), sweet potatoes, and squash
Spinach, tomatoes, and other dark leafy greens
Legumes including lentils, kidney beans, and chickpeas
Dairy products (milk, yoghurt) providing potassium alongside calcium
avoid: Highly processed, sodium-heavy, low-potassium diets that displace potassium-rich whole foods
Excessive alcohol, which increases urinary potassium excretion
Large amounts of coffee or caffeine in those prone to depletion, as it has mild diuretic effects
Liquorice extract products in patients on diuretics or with aldosteronism, as they worsen potassium wasting

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is safe and beneficial in patients with mild or corrected hypokalemia. Patients with moderate to severe hypokalemia (below 3.0 mmol/L) should defer strenuous exercise until the level is corrected, because muscular exertion increases the risk of rhabdomyolysis in the setting of impaired muscle membrane function. Athletes and patients with hypokalemic periodic paralysis should consume electrolyte-containing drinks during and after exercise. Any new episode of muscle weakness during exercise warrants cessation and medical review.
dailyManagement: Take potassium supplements as prescribed — with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects.
Increase fruit and vegetable intake consistently rather than in sporadic high doses.
Monitor for symptoms of over-replacement (muscle cramps, palpitations that may indicate rebound hyperkalemia) and report them.
Report new or worsening muscle weakness, palpitations, constipation, or tingling promptly to a clinician.
Attend scheduled blood tests; potassium levels should be checked every 3-6 months on stable therapy.
prognosis: Mild to moderate hypokalemia treated appropriately carries an excellent prognosis with full recovery of normal potassium levels expected within days to weeks. Severe hypokalemia requiring hospitalisation resolves with intravenous replacement in the large majority of patients. The prognosis is determined more by the underlying cause than the hypokalemia itself: Conn's syndrome adenoma is surgically curable; diuretic-induced hypokalemia is fully preventable; eating-disorder-associated hypokalemia recurs until the eating disorder is treated. The key prognostic concern is cardiac arrhythmia: potassium below 3.0 mmol/L in a patient with underlying heart disease, particularly those on digoxin, significantly elevates the risk of ventricular arrhythmia. Maintaining potassium above 4.0 mmol/L in patients with heart failure or arrhythmia-prone conditions is associated with reduced cardiovascular mortality.
recoveryTimeline: Mild hypokalemia with adequate oral supplementation and dietary optimisation typically corrects within 3-7 days. Moderate hypokalemia requiring higher-dose oral or IV replacement normalises within 24-72 hours in hospital. Severe hypokalemia requiring large-volume IV infusion typically normalises serum levels in 24-48 hours, though total body repletion takes longer and requires continued oral supplementation after discharge. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis episodes typically resolve within hours with potassium replacement.
complications: Ventricular arrhythmia (torsades de pointes, ventricular fibrillation) — the most dangerous complication, particularly in patients on digoxin or with underlying structural heart disease; potassium below 3.0 mmol/L in this group warrants urgent correction.

Rhabdomyolysis — severe hypokalemia below 2.0 mmol/L can cause widespread muscle cell breakdown, releasing myoglobin into the circulation, which causes acute kidney injury; presents as severe weakness with dark urine.

Paralytic ileus — profound smooth muscle dysfunction from severe hypokalemia produces functional bowel obstruction; a potentially serious complication requiring nasogastric decompression alongside electrolyte correction.

Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — chronic hypokalemia damages renal tubular aquaporin-2 channels, impairing water reabsorption and causing polyuria; usually reversible with correction of potassium levels over weeks.

Rebound hyperkalemia — overly rapid or excessive IV potassium replacement can overshoot; cardiac monitoring and regular serum checks every 2-4 hours during IV replacement are mandatory to prevent this.

supportResources.name: NHS — Hypokalaemia (low potassium levels)
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guide to low potassium including symptoms, causes, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Potassium and CKD
supportResources.description: Patient resource on potassium management for kidney disease patients including dietary guidance.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Minerals and Heart Health
supportResources.description: Overview of potassium and other minerals in cardiovascular health.

sources.title: Gennari FJ. Hypokalemia. New England Journal of Medicine, 1998
Unwin RJ, Luft FC, Shirley DG. Pathophysiology and management of hypokalemia. Nature Reviews Nephrology, 2011
Funder JW et al. The Management of Primary Aldosteronism. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2016
Weir MR, Rolfe M. Potassium homeostasis and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors. Clinical Journal of the American Society of Nephrology, 2010
Kardalas E et al. Hypokalemia: a clinical update. Endocrine Connections, 2018
WHO — Diarrhoeal disease: Management and prevention
Aronson PS, Giebisch G. Effects of pH on potassium: new explanations for old observations. Journal of the American Society of Nephrology, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperkalemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both conditions cause muscle weakness and ECG changes, but hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves and QRS widening rather than T-wave flattening and U waves. Treatment is opposite: potassium removal versus potassium replacement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypovolemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypovolemia causes weakness, dizziness, and tachycardia from volume depletion. It can co-occur with hypokalemia but is diagnosed by vital sign changes and dehydration markers rather than serum potassium or ECG changes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AKI from rhabdomyolysis can be caused by severe hypokalemia, but AKI from other causes typically produces hyperkalemia rather than hypokalemia; the key distinguishing test is serum creatinine trend and urinary potassium excretion pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperkalemia
relatedConditions.relevance: The inverse electrolyte disorder; understanding both is essential when managing diuretics, potassium supplementation, and RAAS inhibitors, as overcorrection swings potassium from one extreme to the other.

relatedConditions.name: Hypovolemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Volume depletion frequently accompanies hypokalemia from gastrointestinal losses; aldosterone activation from hypovolemia independently drives urinary potassium wasting, compounding the deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Diuretic therapy for heart failure is the most common drug cause of hypokalemia; maintaining potassium above 4.0 mmol/L in heart failure patients is associated with reduced ventricular arrhythmia and mortality.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Insulin therapy drives potassium into cells; diabetic diarrhoea, diuretic use, and dietary restriction all compound potassium losses in this population.

relatedConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe hypokalemia below 2.0 mmol/L can cause rhabdomyolysis and secondary AKI; conversely, AKI from volume depletion amplifies ongoing potassium losses from concurrent gastrointestinal illness.

faqs.question: What is hypokalemia and what causes it?
faqs.answer: Hypokalemia means the blood potassium level is below 3.5 mmol/L. The most common causes are diuretic medications (furosemide, hydrochlorothiazide), vomiting, diarrhoea, and primary hyperaldosteronism. In most cases, one or more causes combine to deplete total body potassium stores.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of low potassium?
faqs.answer: Low potassium most commonly causes muscle weakness and cramps, fatigue, constipation, and palpitations. Severe cases produce ascending paralysis, abnormal heartbeat, and polyuria. Mild hypokalemia (3.0-3.5 mmol/L) is often symptom-free.

faqs.question: What potassium level is dangerous?
faqs.answer: A potassium level below 2.5 mmol/L is associated with serious risks including dangerous cardiac arrhythmia, muscle paralysis, and rhabdomyolysis and requires prompt treatment. Levels between 2.5 and 3.0 mmol/L require active management. Even 3.0-3.5 mmol/L significantly raises arrhythmia risk in patients with heart disease.

faqs.question: How is low potassium treated?
faqs.answer: Mild to moderate hypokalemia is treated with oral potassium chloride supplements and dietary improvement. Severe hypokalemia requires intravenous potassium given slowly in hospital. Magnesium deficiency must be corrected simultaneously. The underlying cause is also treated to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Which foods are highest in potassium?
faqs.answer: The richest dietary sources of potassium are avocados, bananas, prunes, potatoes, sweet potatoes, spinach, tomatoes, oranges, and legumes such as lentils and kidney beans. Most adults need 3,500-4,700 mg of potassium per day, roughly equivalent to 5 or more servings of fruit and vegetables.

faqs.question: Can diuretics cause low potassium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Thiazide and loop diuretics are the most common drug cause of hypokalemia. They increase sodium delivery to the distal kidney tubule, driving potassium loss into the urine. Up to 40% of patients on thiazides develop low potassium. Adding a potassium-sparing diuretic or monitoring and supplementing potassium reduces this risk.

faqs.question: Why does vomiting lower potassium?
faqs.answer: Vomiting lowers potassium mainly through an indirect mechanism: vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis and volume depletion, which activates aldosterone. Aldosterone then drives the kidney to waste potassium in the urine. The potassium content of vomitus itself is relatively low; it is the aldosterone-driven renal loss that produces significant depletion.

faqs.question: What is hypokalemic periodic paralysis?
faqs.answer: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis is a condition of episodic severe muscle weakness or paralysis caused by sudden drops in serum potassium as it shifts into cells. It can be genetic or triggered by thyrotoxicosis, particularly in East Asian men. Episodes are often provoked by carbohydrate-rich meals, insulin, or exercise and are treated with oral or IV potassium.

faqs.question: How does low potassium affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Potassium controls the electrical repolarisation of heart muscle cells. When potassium is low, repolarisation is prolonged, producing a characteristic ECG pattern of flat T waves, prominent U waves, and a prolonged QT interval. This creates a risk of torsades de pointes (a dangerous ventricular arrhythmia) and ventricular fibrillation, especially in patients with existing heart disease.

faqs.question: Can low potassium cause permanent kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Chronic severe hypokalemia can impair renal tubular function, producing a form of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and, rarely, hypokalemic nephropathy with interstitial scarring. Both are largely reversible with prompt and sustained correction. Acute rhabdomyolysis from very low potassium can cause acute kidney injury that usually recovers fully with adequate fluid and potassium replacement.

faqs.question: Why does hypokalemia not correct despite giving potassium?
faqs.answer: Refractory hypokalemia despite supplementation is most commonly caused by unrecognised hypomagnesaemia. Magnesium is needed for the Na/K-ATPase pump that retains potassium inside cells; without it, given potassium leaks back out. Other reasons include ongoing losses from unreplaced diarrhoea, persistent diuretic effect, or primary hyperaldosteronism.

faqs.question: Is low potassium related to high blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Primary hyperaldosteronism — the most common endocrine cause of secondary hypertension — simultaneously causes high blood pressure and persistent hypokalemia. Any hypertensive patient with unexplained low potassium should be screened for this condition with a plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio, as it is present in 5-10% of all hypertension cases.

faqs.question: Can eating disorders cause low potassium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Purging behaviours in bulimia nervosa and other eating disorders — self-induced vomiting, laxative abuse, and diuretic misuse — are among the most severe causes of potassium depletion. More than 50% of patients with purging-type eating disorders have measurable hypokalemia. Cardiac arrest from hypokalemia-related arrhythmia is a leading cause of death in this population.

faqs.question: How quickly should potassium be replaced?
faqs.answer: Oral replacement at 40-80 mmol daily is safe for outpatient mild to moderate deficiency. IV replacement is limited to 10-20 mmol/hour via a peripheral line to prevent local irritation and dangerous rebound hyperkalemia. In severe arrhythmia, up to 40 mmol/hour via a central line with continuous ECG monitoring may be used. Faster replacement risks cardiac toxicity.

faqs.question: Does low potassium cause leg cramps?
faqs.answer: Yes. Muscle cramps, particularly nocturnal leg cramps, are a very common symptom of mild to moderate hypokalemia. They result from impaired muscle membrane function at low extracellular potassium concentrations. Correcting the deficiency with supplements and dietary change usually resolves them within days to a week.

faqs.question: Can I take potassium supplements without a doctor?
faqs.answer: Over-the-counter potassium supplements contain low doses (up to 99 mg per tablet in the US) that are unlikely to cause harm but are insufficient for treating true deficiency. Prescription-strength potassium chloride is needed for moderate to severe hypokalemia. Self-treatment without diagnosis risks over-replacement (causing hyperkalemia) or missing a serious underlying cause.

faqs.question: What is the connection between low potassium and digoxin?
faqs.answer: Digoxin toxicity is dramatically worsened by hypokalemia. Low potassium increases the binding of digoxin to its cardiac receptor (Na/K-ATPase) and heightens myocardial sensitivity to digoxin-induced arrhythmia. Any patient on digoxin must have potassium maintained above 4.0 mmol/L; a combination of low potassium and digoxin is one of the most recognised preventable causes of cardiac arrhythmia death.

faqs.question: What does a U wave on an ECG mean?
faqs.answer: A U wave is a positive deflection on the ECG that appears after the T wave, most visible in leads V2-V4. Prominent U waves are a characteristic ECG sign of hypokalemia, reflecting the prolonged cardiac repolarisation caused by low extracellular potassium. A U wave larger than the T wave in the same lead is a reliable indicator of significant hypokalemia.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for low potassium?
faqs.answer: Go to hospital immediately if low potassium is associated with significant muscle weakness, difficulty breathing, severe constipation or abdominal distension, palpitations, or irregular heartbeat. A serum potassium below 2.5 mmol/L requires hospital-based IV replacement. Milder levels without symptoms can be managed in primary care with oral supplements.

faqs.question: Does low potassium cause fatigue?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fatigue is one of the most common symptoms of hypokalemia, often one of the first to appear. Reduced muscle cell energy production and impaired nerve signalling at low potassium concentrations create a pervasive feeling of tiredness and reduced stamina. Fatigue typically improves within a week of adequate potassium correction.

metaDescription: Hypokalemia means low blood potassium causing muscle weakness, cramps and heart arrhythmia. Learn causes, diagnosis and potassium replacement. Doctor-reviewed.
